title: Jennifer Lawrence's Movie Star Playbook and 'No Hard Feelings'
format: Amanda Dobbins takes the chair from an under-the-weather Sean Fennessey to review the sex comedy 'No Hard Feelings,' then draws up a Jennifer Lawrence career playbook (where her comic instinct got lost and who should direct her next) before closing on the Academy's new theatrical-eligibility rule.

films.title: No Hard Feelings
films.note: Gene Stupnitsky's Montauk comedy: a 32-year-old Uber driver (Lawrence) is hired by wealthy parents, in exchange for a car, to draw out their sheltered 19-year-old (Andrew Barth Feldman) before college. Both hosts had fun without calling it great: a throwback studio comedy that plays broad in the first third and turns feelings-forward, with a rickety third act. They flag it as the first correct use of Lawrence's comic talents and single out Feldman, whose presence keeps the premise from curdling.

segments.heading: The Jennifer Lawrence Playbook
segments.note: the career, and what she should do next

films.title: Winter's Bone
films.note: The debut that got her a first Oscar nomination; Sean pegs the 2010-11 stretch of grounded, stripped-down work as her strongest dramatic run.

films.title: Like Crazy
films.note: Named alongside Winter's Bone and The Beaver as the grounded early run Sean rates highest.

films.title: The Beaver
films.note: The third title in that early grounded stretch.

films.title: The Hunger Games
films.note: The franchise that actually made her a star. Sean never bought Katniss (he thought she was a decade too young) but concedes it made sense as a YA adaptation not aimed at him.

films.title: Silver Linings Playbook
films.note: The Oscar win and the movie that signaled the rising star. Sean's dissent: he finds her woefully miscast, ten years too young for the trauma the part needed opposite Bradley Cooper.

films.title: American Hustle
films.note: The 'acting Olympics' David O. Russell movie both admit they don't get; Amanda thinks she's so much younger than Adams, Bale, Cooper and Renner that the miscasting shows.

films.title: Joy
films.note: The QVC-mop-inventor drama, playing a Peconic housewife older than Lawrence (then about 24 or 25) actually was. Stiff and retread, per Sean, though Amanda owns some genuine Joy Mangano hangers.

films.title: Passengers
films.note: Called her biggest fiasco, opposite Chris Pratt, though on paper it made sense as Morten Tyldum's follow-up to The Imitation Game.

films.title: mother!
films.note: The Aronofsky provocation. Sean loves it as a filmmaker jabbing the audience with a stick and reads it as knowing meta-commentary on Lawrence's younger-wife-of-an-older-man casting; he argues Natalie Portman could have played it just as well.

films.title: Red Sparrow
films.note: Her bid for a Bourne-style franchise, from a reportedly filthy spy novel. Doesn't quite work, but Jeremy Irons and a Charlotte Rampling turn keep Sean from getting mad at it.

films.title: Dark Phoenix
films.note: Called one of the century's fiascos; Sean reads her as fully checked out, her character killed off early as if she asked to leave set.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: The successful Adam McKay satire they opted out of the discourse on. Both agree the Lawrence piece doesn't land, but Sean likes the sincere ending and thinks she and McKay were a good idea: they'd once eyed an Elizabeth Holmes film together before The Dropout beat them to it.

films.title: Causeway
films.note: The Lila Neugebauer drama with Brian Tyree Henry; her most recent pre-comedy role and an Oscar-nominated one. Small, quiet and, per Sean, genuinely good: proof the experiments are worth keeping in the mix.

films.title: To Leslie
films.note: Sean's test case: a Best Actress nominee that played almost nowhere, then climbed the Netflix charts on award-season publicity, the kind of tiny film the new rule could squeeze.

films.title: Air
films.note: Cited as the counterexample: it played roughly 3,500 screens, so the streamers with money to spend can clear the bar easily.

films.title: Napoleon
films.note: Named with Killers of the Flower Moon as an Apple title already headed for thousands of fall screens; the rule barely inconveniences the deep-pocketed.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: The other Apple fall release cited as proof the well-funded streamers can meet the requirement without strain.
